data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "No fixed (base error, precision, recall, k) regime is sufficient to predict strict harness advantage; the determining factor is question-conditional alignment between critic confidence and candidate-answer correctness, measurable only via a conditional intervention that varies the critic's error-correlation with the generator's error.", "mechanism": "Even if base error is in a mid-zone and critic precision/recall look favorable on average, the critic's false flags and true flags may be correlated with the same questions where the generator's majority is already correct or already wrong. If the critic is systematically miscalibrated on exactly the majority-fragile questions, a favorable average precision can still produce net break > fix. Strict harness advantage requires not just favorable average precision but conditional precision on the fix-opportunity set (W) exceeding conditional precision on the break-risk set (M). The measured data showed critic precision 0.125 at recall 1.0 on the hard set, which is compatible with the critic flagging almost everything on questions where the generator was already correct, producing zero net gain.", "scope": {"covers": ["k in {5, 7, 9}", "base error in [0.05, 0.40]", "critics whose flag distribution is question-conditional, not just answer-conditional", "experiments that measure conditional precision P(flag|majority fragile and correct) vs P(flag|majority fragile and wrong)"]}, "excludes": ["claims that fixed average precision/recall thresholds suffice to predict harness advantage", "base error < 0.05: insufficient variation to measure conditional alignment", "critics that see the answer key (violates held-out condition)"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "At two different critic configurations with identical average precision ~0.5 and recall ~0.5 but different conditional alignment, harness advantage differs by more than 5 percentage points", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Harness does not strictly beat SC at any tested (e, p, r, k) despite conditional alignment being favorable, i.e., conditional precision on W exceeding conditional precision on M by >= 0.2", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "The intervention that flips critic alignment (e.g., different prompt framing) produces no measurable change in fix-minus-break despite measurable change in conditional precision", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "This candidate is more atypical because it denies that a simple regime map suffices and insists on a conditional/interaction measurement. The decisive experiment would use two critic variants (e.g., 'skeptical' vs 'charitable' framing) that produce different flag distributions over the same candidate pool, allowing measurement of whether conditional alignment, not average precision, drives harness advantage. Budget: 300k tokens suffices for ~500 questions x 5 candidates x 2 critic variants x 1 generation pass."}
